extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine. The property requires that globally (opted_out implies not sent), i.e., no reachable state has opted_out=true AND sent=true.

Scanning the transitions for ones that set sent:=true: t2 fires only when NOT opted_out (safe at that moment), and t5 fires on send_attempt when (opted_out AND ticks>=12) and sets sent:=true. t5 is the violating transition: it sets sent=true precisely while opted_out is true.

Reachability: ticks starts at 0 and is incremented by t4 (tick) up to a cap of 12, so 12 tick events drive ticks to 12. Then optout (t1) sets opted_out=true, sent=false. A subsequent send_attempt enables t5 (guard opted_out and ticks>=12 holds), applying sent:=true while opted_out remains true. This yields the state opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=12, which violates opted_out implies not sent.

Counterexample (14 events, within the 16-event bound): 12x tick (ticks 0->12), then optout (opted_out=true, sent=false), then send_attempt (t5 fires: sent:=true). Final violating state: opted_out=true AND sent=true.

Note on priority: on send_attempt with opted_out=true and ticks>=12, both t3 (sets sent:=false) and t5 (sets sent:=true) are enabled. t5 is deliberately crafted to set sent=true in exactly this case; under the standard reading that any enabled transition may fire, t5's execution is reachable and produces the violating state. The property is violated.
